enrichment.short_description: Proposify is a cloud-based proposal and document automation platform that helps sales teams design, send, track, and electronically sign business proposals, quotes, and contracts.
enrichment.pricing_model: Subscription (per-user, monthly or annual billing) with a free trial
enrichment.starting_price: From $19/user/month (Basic plan, billed annually; $29/user/month billed monthly)
enrichment.seo_title: Proposify Review 2026: Pricing, Features, Pros and Cons
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Proposify costs in 2026, how its proposal software works, and whether its Basic, Team, and Business plans fit your sales workflow.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Proposify is a proposal management and document automation platform founded in 2013 by Kyle Racki and Kevin Springer in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada. The company grew out of an earlier idea Racki had in 2007 while running a web design agency, and after securing a seed investment the pair built Proposify into a dedicated proposal-software business, later raising additional funding including a $3 million round in 2018 and investment from CBGF in 2020. As of its most recent public figures, Proposify employs around 60 people, serves more than 8,000 customers, and generates over $10 million in annual recurring revenue. The product lets sales and business-development teams build interactive, on-brand proposals, quotes, and contracts using a drag-and-drop editor, then send them for electronic signature and track exactly when and how a prospect views the document. Proposify markets itself primarily to mid-market and enterprise sales organizations that want to standardize proposal content, reduce the time reps spend assembling documents, and shorten the gap between a signed deal and a sent proposal. Pricing is structured across four tiers. The Basic plan costs $19 per user per month billed annually (or $29 per user per month billed monthly) and covers core proposal creation, e-signatures, and document tracking with 10 document sends per month. The Team plan, aimed at growing teams that need CRM integration, costs $41 per user per month billed annually or $49 per user per month billed quarterly, and adds document analytics, integrations with CRMs such as HubSpot, Pipedrive, and Zoho, custom fields and variables, and phone and Zoom support. The Business plan is custom-priced starting at $3,900 per year and adds role-based permissions, single sign-on via Okta, Azure, or Salesforce, API access, multiple workspaces, and a dedicated success manager; Salesforce integration is a paid add-on available only on this tier. An Enterprise tier is available on request for organizations needing further customization and support. All plans include a 14-day free trial with no credit card required, unlimited document creation, AI-assisted writing tools, and unlimited contacts. Proposify holds SOC 2 Type II certification, is GDPR compliant, and processes payments through PCI-DSS-compliant infrastructure via Stripe. In 2024 the company marked its ten-year product anniversary and began rolling out Proposify 3.0, a rebuilt version of the platform focused on speed and reliability. Competing directly with tools like PandaDoc, Qwilr, and Better Proposals, Proposify differentiates itself with deep proposal-specific analytics (such as time-per-section tracking) and a design-forward editor built for sales teams rather than general-purpose e-signature workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Proposify is a purpose-built proposal software company founded in 2013 in Halifax, Nova Scotia, by Kyle Racki and Kevin Springer. Rather than functioning as a generic e-signature or document-sharing tool, Proposify is designed specifically around the sales proposal workflow: assembling branded content blocks, pricing tables, and legal terms into a single interactive document that a prospect can review, comment on, and sign online.

The platform has grown to serve more than 8,000 customers and crossed $10 million in annual recurring revenue, with the company celebrating its tenth product anniversary in 2024 alongside the rollout of a rebuilt Proposify 3.0 experience focused on speed and reliability.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Proposify offers a drag-and-drop content editor with reusable templates and content blocks, so sales reps can assemble a polished proposal from approved, on-brand sections rather than starting from a blank document each time. Built-in electronic signatures let prospects sign directly inside the document, and real-time tracking shows sales teams exactly when a proposal was opened, which sections were read, and how long a viewer spent on pricing.

Higher tiers add CRM integrations (HubSpot, Pipedrive, Zoho, and Salesforce as an add-on), interactive pricing tables that let buyers select add-ons and see totals update live, role-based permissions, single sign-on, and an API for connecting Proposify into a broader sales stack. AI-assisted writing tools are available across all plans to help reps draft or refine proposal copy faster.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Proposify prices per user across four tiers. Basic starts at $19/user/month billed annually ($29/user/month billed monthly) and includes 10 document sends per month with overage billed at $0.50 per send. Team costs $41/user/month billed annually ($49/user/month billed quarterly) and is aimed at teams that need CRM automation and unlimited sends. Business starts at $3,900/year with custom pricing and adds enterprise controls like SSO and API access, while Enterprise pricing is fully custom.
Every plan includes a 14-day free trial with no credit card required, giving prospective customers access to Team-level features before committing to a paid plan.

features.title: CRM integrations
features.description: Native and Zapier-based integrations with HubSpot, Pipedrive, Zoho, and Salesforce (as a paid add-on) sync proposal data back to the deal record.

features.title: AI writing assistance
features.description: AI tools help sales reps draft, rewrite, and refine proposal copy directly inside the editor.

features.description: Business and Enterprise customers can connect Proposify to internal tools and custom workflows via API.

Detailed document analytics show exactly how prospects engage with a proposal
Interactive, on-brand pricing tables help increase average deal size
Strong CRM integrations on the Team plan and above
SOC 2 Type II certified and GDPR compliant
cons: Per-user pricing can get expensive for larger sales teams
Salesforce integration is a paid add-on only available on the Business plan
Document send limits and overage fees apply on lower-tier plans
Business and Enterprise pricing requires contacting sales rather than transparent self-serve pricing

faqs.question: What does Proposify cost?
faqs.answer: Proposify's Basic plan starts at $19/user/month billed annually or $29/user/month billed monthly. The Team plan is $41/user/month billed annually, and the Business plan starts at $3,900/year with custom pricing.

faqs.question: Does Proposify offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Proposify offers a 14-day free trial with access to Team-level features and no credit card required.

faqs.question: Can Proposify integrate with my CRM?
faqs.answer: Yes, Proposify integrates with CRMs including HubSpot, Pipedrive, and Zoho on the Team plan and above, with Salesforce available as a paid add-on on the Business plan.

faqs.question: Does Proposify support electronic signatures?
faqs.answer: Yes, e-signatures are included on every plan, allowing prospects to sign proposals directly inside the document.

faqs.question: Who founded Proposify?
faqs.answer: Proposify was founded in 2013 by Kyle Racki and Kevin Springer in Halifax, Nova Scotia, Canada.

faqs.question: Is Proposify SOC 2 certified?
faqs.answer: Yes, Proposify is SOC 2 Type II certified, GDPR compliant, and processes payments through PCI-DSS-compliant infrastructure.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Proposify's Team and Business plans?
faqs.answer: The Business plan adds role-based permissions, single sign-on, API access, multiple workspaces, and a dedicated success manager on top of everything included in the Team plan.

use_cases.title: Sales teams standardizing proposal content

use_cases.description: Larger organizations use Business and Enterprise plans to control who can edit templates and pricing via roles, permissions, and SSO.

enrichment.short_description: ProProfs Help Desk is a shared-inbox ticketing platform that helps support teams manage customer emails, track tickets, automate workflows, and measure satisfaction from a single dashboard.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium with per-seat monthly or annual subscription tiers
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans start around $29.99/month per user (lower with annual billing)
enrichment.headquarters: Santa Monica/Los Angeles, California, United States
languages: additional languages supported via ProProfs Knowledge Base integration
enrichment.seo_title: ProProfs Help Desk Review 2026: Pricing, Features, Pros and Cons
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore ProProfs Help Desk pricing, ticketing features, and AI tools in 2026, and see if its shared-inbox plans fit your support team.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ProProfs Help Desk is a ticketing and shared-inbox support platform built by ProProfs, a company founded in 2005 and headquartered in the Santa Monica/Los Angeles area of California. ProProfs operates a broader suite of SaaS tools spanning training, quizzes, surveys, live chat, and knowledge base software, with Help Desk being one of several products under that umbrella; the parent company reports roughly 200 employees across its full product line. Help Desk itself is aimed at small and mid-sized customer support teams that want shared email inboxes, ticket assignment, and basic automation without the complexity or cost of larger enterprise service-desk platforms. The product's free plan supports a single agent, unlimited inboxes and ticket history, and up to 100 tickets per month, making it usable for very small teams or solo founders handling their own support. Paid tiers scale up in ticket volume and automation depth. The Essentials plan is priced at $29.99 per user per month (with lower effective per-user annual pricing reported by third-party sources) and includes 1,000 tickets per month, AI-assisted response drafts, ticket categorization, and grammar checking. The Business plan, also listed at $29.99 per month, raises the ticket ceiling to 6,000 per month and adds AI-generated first responses, tone rewriting, unlimited inboxes, custom ticket forms, and omnichannel support across email and web forms. The top self-serve tier, Business Complete Elite, costs $179.97 per month ($719.88 annually) for three user seats and 15,000 tickets per month, adding omnichannel channels like WhatsApp, Facebook, Instagram, SMS, and Telegram, 40-plus integrations, API access, SSO/SAML, AES-256 encryption, and a dedicated account manager. For organizations wanting ProProfs' full customer-experience stack, the Customer Delight Suite starts at $499 per month and bundles Help Desk with Live Chat, Knowledge Base, Survey Maker, and Qualaroo, with ProProfs claiming its AI can auto-resolve more than half of incoming tickets. All paid plans are backed by a 15-day money-back guarantee. ProProfs Help Desk competes with tools like Freshdesk, Zoho Desk, and Help Scout, and it differentiates primarily through tight integration with ProProfs' other support products (chat, knowledge base, and surveys) and an emphasis on straightforward per-agent pricing rather than complex enterprise licensing. The company markets the product heavily toward small businesses and teams migrating off shared email inboxes like Gmail or Outlook groups for the first time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ProProfs Help Desk is a ticketing and shared-inbox platform built by ProProfs, a company founded in 2005 and based in the Santa Monica/Los Angeles area of California. Help Desk is one product within a wider ProProfs suite that also includes live chat, knowledge base, quiz, and survey software, and it is aimed primarily at small and mid-sized teams that need to organize customer email into trackable tickets without adopting a heavier enterprise service-desk platform.

The product emphasizes a shared-inbox model where multiple agents can see, claim, and respond to the same support email address, paired with automation rules, canned responses, and increasingly AI-assisted drafting and categorization as customers move up the pricing tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core ticketing features include shared inboxes, ticket tagging and labeling, canned responses, ticket history, and mobile app access, all available starting on the free plan. Paid tiers add AI response drafts, automatic ticket categorization, grammar checking, and eventually AI-generated first responses with tone rewriting on the Business plan.

Higher tiers introduce omnichannel support across WhatsApp, Facebook, Instagram, SMS, and Telegram, custom ticket forms, smart workflow automation, CSAT survey tracking, and enterprise controls like SSO/SAML and AES-256 encryption on the Business Complete Elite plan. ProProfs also integrates Help Desk with its own Knowledge Base, Live Chat, and Survey Maker products for teams that want a combined support stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ProProfs Help Desk offers a permanent free plan for a single agent with up to 100 tickets per month. Paid plans start with Essentials at $29.99 per user per month, followed by Business at the same list price of $29.99 per month but with a higher ticket ceiling and more automation, and Business Complete Elite at $179.97 per month ($719.88 billed annually) for three seats.

Organizations that want ProProfs' full support stack can opt for the Customer Delight Suite starting at $499 per month, which bundles Help Desk with Live Chat, Knowledge Base, Survey Maker, and Qualaroo under unlimited users. All paid plans include a 15-day money-back guarantee.

features.title: Shared team inbox
features.description: Multiple agents work from the same support email address with ticket assignment and internal collision detection.

features.title: Automation and workflow rules
features.description: Automatically route, tag, and escalate tickets based on rules so agents spend less time on manual triage.

features.title: CSAT surveys and reporting
features.description: Built-in customer satisfaction surveys and reporting dashboards track team performance and resolution quality.

features.title: Knowledge base integration
features.description: Connects with ProProfs Knowledge Base so agents can deflect tickets by linking self-service articles.

features.title: Enterprise security controls
features.description: Business Complete Elite adds SSO/SAML, AES-256 encryption, and role-based access for larger, security-conscious teams.

features.title: Mobile app access
features.description: Agents can view and respond to tickets from a native mobile app, available even on the free plan.

pros: Free plan is usable for real, low-volume support teams, not just a trial
AI features (response drafts, tone rewriting, categorization) are included well below enterprise pricing
Tight integration with other ProProfs tools like Knowledge Base and Live Chat
15-day money-back guarantee on paid plans
Omnichannel support (WhatsApp, SMS, social) available without moving to top-tier enterprise pricing
cons: Ticket volume caps on lower tiers can be limiting for growing teams
Essentials and Business plans list at the same $29.99 price, which can be confusing when comparing features
Advanced automation and integrations are gated behind the higher-cost Elite tier
Company-wide employee count spans ProProfs' full product suite, not Help Desk specifically

faqs.question: Is there a free version of ProProfs Help Desk?
faqs.answer: Yes, ProProfs Help Desk offers a free plan for one agent with unlimited inboxes and ticket history and up to 100 tickets per month.

faqs.question: How much does ProProfs Help Desk cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $29.99 per user per month for the Essentials plan, scaling up to $179.97/month for Business Complete Elite and from $499/month for the bundled Customer Delight Suite.

faqs.question: Does ProProfs Help Desk include AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans include AI response drafts, automatic ticket categorization, grammar checking, and (on Business and above) AI-generated first responses with tone rewriting.

faqs.question: Can ProProfs Help Desk handle social media and messaging channels?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Business Complete Elite plan adds omnichannel support for WhatsApp, Facebook, Instagram, SMS, and Telegram alongside email.

faqs.question: When was ProProfs founded?
faqs.answer: ProProfs was founded in 2005 and is headquartered in the Santa Monica/Los Angeles area of California.

faqs.question: Does ProProfs Help Desk offer a money-back guarantee?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans include a 15-day money-back guarantee.

faqs.question: Does ProProfs Help Desk integrate with a knowledge base?
faqs.answer: Yes, it integrates with ProProfs Knowledge Base so agents can share self-service articles and deflect repetitive tickets.

enrichment.short_description: ProRankTracker is a cloud-based SEO rank tracking tool that monitors keyword positions across search engines, local maps, and AI search results, with reporting built for agencies and in-house SEO teams.
enrichment.pricing_model: Subscription (monthly or annual per-plan pricing based on tracked terms)
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (small, privately held company)
enrichment.seo_title: ProRankTracker Review 2026: Pricing, Features, Pros and Cons
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare ProRankTracker's Starter, Pro, and Agency pricing, keyword tracking features, and AI search monitoring tools for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ProRankTracker is a cloud-based search engine rank tracking platform founded around 2012 by Eyal Aftabi, who continues to serve as its founder and CEO. The company is based in Tel Aviv, Israel, and operates as a privately held, independently run business rather than a venture-backed startup; its exact employee count is not publicly disclosed, consistent with many bootstrapped SEO tool vendors. ProRankTracker's core product tracks keyword rankings across desktop and mobile search results, Google Business Profile map listings, and, more recently, AI-driven answer surfaces such as AI Overviews, AI Mode, and large-language-model platforms, positioning it to compete not only with traditional rank trackers like SEMrush and Ahrefs' tracking modules but also with newer 'AI visibility' tracking tools. The platform serves two main audiences: in-house SEO practitioners tracking their own site's rankings, and digital marketing agencies that need white-label reporting and multi-client dashboards. Pricing is structured around the number of tracked terms rather than flat per-seat fees. A free trial offers 100 terms tracked daily for seven days with all premium features unlocked and no credit card required. The Starter plan costs $99/month (or $89/month billed annually, a 10 percent discount) and tracks 1,000 terms with daily rank updates, local and mobile tracking, Google Business Profile map tracking, and access to AI Overview and AI platform tracking. The Pro plan costs $149/month ($134/month annual) and doubles term capacity to 2,000 while adding Looker Studio integration, a client-facing MyRanks app, support for 10 users, and unlimited reports. The Agency plan is the most flexible tier, scaling from 3,000 to more than 20,000 tracked terms with monthly pricing ranging from roughly $199 up to $1,039 or more (also discounted 10 percent on annual billing), and it adds API access, on-demand rank updates, unlimited users, and a dedicated customer success manager; custom quotes are available for agencies tracking beyond 20,000 terms. ProRankTracker accepts multiple payment methods including cards, PayPal, and Amazon Pay, imposes no long-term contracts, and allows cancellation at any time, though downgrades are non-refundable for the current billing period. Because of its focus on granular local, mobile, and AI-search tracking alongside traditional organic rank monitoring, ProRankTracker is frequently positioned by reviewers as a mid-market alternative to larger all-in-one SEO suites for teams that specifically need deep rank-tracking capability rather than a full toolkit covering backlinks, content, and site audits.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ProRankTracker is a dedicated search engine rank tracking platform founded around 2012 by Eyal Aftabi and headquartered in Tel Aviv, Israel. Unlike broader all-in-one SEO suites, ProRankTracker focuses specifically on monitoring where a website's pages rank for target keywords, across desktop and mobile search, local map listings, and, increasingly, AI-generated search answers.

The platform is used both by individual SEO practitioners tracking a single site and by digital marketing agencies managing rankings across many client accounts, with tiered plans and white-label-style reporting built for that agency use case.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ProRankTracker tracks keyword positions with daily updates across the top 100 search results, including local and mobile-specific rankings and Google Business Profile map positions. More recently the platform added tracking for AI Overviews, Google's AI Mode, and other AI/LLM search platforms, reflecting the shift toward AI-driven search answers alongside traditional blue-link results.

Higher-tier plans add reporting and workflow features aimed at agencies: Looker Studio integration for custom dashboards, a client-facing MyRanks app that lets agencies share live rank data with customers, unlimited saved reports, API access for pulling data into other systems, and on-demand rank checks ("insta-checks") outside the normal daily update cycle.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ProRankTracker prices plans primarily by the number of tracked terms rather than by user seats. The Starter plan is $99/month ($89/month billed annually) for 1,000 terms, the Pro plan is $149/month ($134/month annually) for 2,000 terms plus agency-oriented features, and the Agency plan scales from 3,000 up to 20,000+ terms with monthly pricing from about $199 to $1,039 or more, discounted 10 percent on annual billing.
A 7-day free trial with 100 tracked terms and all premium features unlocked is available with no credit card required, and the service can be cancelled at any time with no long-term contract.

features.title: Daily keyword rank tracking
features.description: Tracks positions in the top 100 search results daily across desktop and mobile for unlimited URLs and projects.

features.title: Local and Google Business Profile tracking
features.description: Monitors local pack and map rankings tied to a business's Google Business Profile listing.

features.title: AI search visibility tracking
features.description: Tracks a site's visibility in AI Overviews, Google AI Mode, and other AI/LLM-based search platforms.

features.title: White-label client reporting
features.description: The MyRanks client app and unlimited reports let agencies share branded, live rank data with their own clients.

features.title: Looker Studio integration
features.description: Pro and Agency plans connect ranking data directly into Google Looker Studio for custom dashboards.

features.title: On-demand rank checks
features.description: "Insta-check" credits let users pull fresh rank data outside the normal daily update schedule.

features.title: Multi-user and API access
features.description: Pro supports up to 10 users; Agency plans include unlimited users and API access for custom integrations.

pros: Granular local, mobile, and Google Business Profile rank tracking
Early mover in tracking AI Overviews and AI/LLM search visibility
Flexible term-based pricing that scales with tracking needs
No long-term contracts and a no-credit-card-required free trial
White-label reporting suited to agencies managing multiple clients
cons: Pricing based on tracked terms can get expensive quickly for large keyword sets
No published free-forever plan, only a 7-day trial
Company employee count and financials are not publicly disclosed
Primarily an English-language interface, which may limit non-English-speaking teams

faqs.question: How much does ProRankTracker cost?
faqs.answer: Plans start at $99/month for the Starter tier ($89/month billed annually), rising to $149/month for Pro and from $199 up to $1,039+/month for Agency plans depending on the number of tracked terms.

faqs.question: Does ProRankTracker offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, a 7-day free trial with 100 tracked terms and all premium features is available with no credit card required.

faqs.question: Can ProRankTracker track AI search results?
faqs.answer: Yes, ProRankTracker tracks visibility in AI Overviews, Google's AI Mode, and other AI/LLM search platforms in addition to traditional organic rankings.

faqs.question: Who owns ProRankTracker?
faqs.answer: ProRankTracker was founded by Eyal Aftabi, who remains the company's founder and CEO, based in Tel Aviv, Israel.

faqs.question: Does ProRankTracker support agencies with multiple clients?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Agency plan includes unlimited users, API access, and a client-facing MyRanks app for white-label style reporting.

faqs.question: Is there a long-term contract required for ProRankTracker?
faqs.answer: No, ProRankTracker has no long-term contracts and can be cancelled at any time, though downgrades are non-refundable for the current period.

faqs.question: What is a "term" in ProRankTracker pricing?
faqs.answer: For organic tracking, a term is one keyword-URL-search-engine combination; for AI tracking, one prompt plus URL plus platform/location combination counts as two terms.

use_cases.title: In-house SEO teams tracking core keywords
use_cases.description: Marketing teams use the Starter or Pro plan to monitor daily ranking changes for their most important target keywords.
use_cases.audience: In-house SEO specialists and marketing teams

use_cases.description: SEO and digital marketing agencies use the Agency plan's white-label MyRanks app and unlimited reports to share live results with clients.

use_cases.title: Local businesses monitoring map visibility
use_cases.description: Multi-location or service-area businesses use local and Google Business Profile tracking to monitor map pack rankings by location.
use_cases.audience: Local businesses and franchise marketing teams

features: AI Overviews and AI platform tracking

enrichment.short_description: Prosody IM is a free, open-source XMPP (Jabber) instant messaging server designed for low resource usage, ease of configuration, and extensibility, used to self-host secure chat infrastructure.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source (no paid tiers; optional paid third-party hosting and support exist in the ecosystem)
enrichment.company_size: Not applicable (open-source project maintained by a volunteer community, not a company)
enrichment.headquarters: Not applicable (distributed open-source project; lead developer based in the United Kingdom)
languages: English (documentation and configuration); protocol-level support for internationalized messaging via XMPP
enrichment.seo_title: Prosody IM Review 2026: Features, Setup, Pros and Cons
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what Prosody IM offers as a free, open-source XMPP chat server, including its features, extensibility, and how it compares for self-hosting.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Prosody IM is a free and open-source XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol, also known as Jabber) server used to self-host real-time instant messaging infrastructure. Development began in August 2008, led by developer Matthew Wild, who is based in the United Kingdom; the first public release, version 0.1.0, shipped in December 2008. The project was initially licensed under the GNU General Public License version 2 and later moved to the MIT License starting with its third release, reflecting the broader open-source community's preference for permissive licensing. Prosody is written in the Lua programming language, a design choice made specifically to keep the server lightweight, fast to configure, and easy to extend through Lua-based plugins and modules. Because it is an open-source project rather than a commercial company, Prosody has no headquarters, no employee count, and no venture funding; it is maintained by a distributed community of contributors coordinated primarily through the project's website, documentation wiki, community chatrooms, and code repository. There is no pricing model in the traditional sense: Prosody is free to download, deploy, and modify under its open-source license, and any costs a user incurs come from the infrastructure (servers, bandwidth) they choose to run it on, or from optional third-party hosting and consulting services offered by companies in the wider XMPP ecosystem, not from Prosody itself. Prosody's core design goals are low resource usage, straightforward configuration for system administrators, and a modular architecture that allows features to be added or removed via plugins rather than baked into a monolithic core. It supports the standard set of XMPP extensions (XEPs) needed for modern chat, including multi-user chatrooms (groupchats), message archive management, push notifications for mobile clients, and end-to-end encryption support through protocols like OMEMO when paired with compatible clients. Notably, the XMPP Standards Foundation itself runs Prosody to power xmpp.org's own chat infrastructure, including chatrooms used for XSF team meetings, which speaks to the project's reputation for stability and standards compliance within the XMPP community. Prosody powers a significant share of the public XMPP federation; various community estimates place it as the server software behind roughly half of publicly reachable XMPP servers, though exact market-share figures are not tracked by any authoritative body and should be treated as community estimates rather than verified statistics. It is commonly deployed by privacy-focused organizations, hobbyists, and businesses that want to run their own secure, federated chat server rather than depending on a closed commercial platform, and it underpins related projects such as Snikket, a simplified self-hosted chat distribution also created by Matthew Wild that packages Prosody with sensible defaults for non-technical users.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Prosody IM is a free, open-source XMPP (Jabber) server that lets individuals and organizations self-host their own real-time instant messaging infrastructure instead of relying on a third-party commercial chat platform. Development began in August 2008 under lead developer Matthew Wild, based in the United Kingdom, with the first public release (0.1.0) shipping in December 2008.

Written in the Lua programming language, Prosody was built around three explicit goals: low resource usage, ease of configuration for system administrators, and extensibility through a modular plugin system. It is maintained by a volunteer open-source community rather than a company, and the XMPP Standards Foundation itself runs Prosody to power the official xmpp.org chat infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Prosody implements the core XMPP protocol along with widely used extensions (XEPs), including multi-user chatrooms, message archive management for chat history, and mobile push notification support, all configurable through a relatively simple Lua-based configuration file rather than a complex enterprise admin console.

Its plugin architecture lets administrators add or remove functionality as needed, from authentication backends to encryption support such as OMEMO when paired with compatible clients. Because it is self-hosted and federated like email, a Prosody server can communicate with any other standards-compliant XMPP server, and the project also underpins Snikket, a simplified Prosody-based distribution aimed at non-technical self-hosters.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Prosody itself is completely free to download, deploy, and modify under its open-source license (originally GPLv2, later MIT starting with its third release). There is no subscription, per-user fee, or paid tier from the project itself.

Any cost associated with running Prosody comes from the infrastructure an organization chooses to host it on (a VPS, dedicated server, or cloud instance) or from optional paid support and hosting offered by third-party companies within the broader XMPP ecosystem, not from Prosody itself.

features.title: Lightweight, low-resource design
features.description: Built to run efficiently even on modest hardware, making it practical for small self-hosted deployments as well as larger installations.

features.title: Lua-based configuration and plugins
features.description: Administrators configure the server and extend functionality through Lua scripts and a modular plugin system.

features.title: Multi-user chatrooms (groupchats)
features.description: Supports XMPP group chat extensions for team and community chatrooms, including the rooms used for XMPP Standards Foundation meetings.

features.title: Message archive management
features.description: Stores and syncs chat history across a user's devices in line with XMPP archiving standards.

features.title: Mobile push notifications
features.description: Supports push notification extensions so mobile XMPP clients can alert users of new messages in real time.

features.title: Federation with other XMPP servers
features.description: Like email, a Prosody server can exchange messages with any other standards-compliant XMPP server without requiring both parties to use Prosody.

features.title: Encryption support
features.description: Works with end-to-end encryption protocols such as OMEMO when used alongside compatible XMPP clients.

features.title: Powers the Snikket distribution
features.description: Serves as the underlying server for Snikket, a simplified, easier-to-deploy self-hosted chat package built on Prosody.

pros: Completely free and open source with no licensing fees
Low resource usage makes it viable on inexpensive or self-managed hardware
Highly extensible through a Lua-based plugin architecture
Federated like email, avoiding lock-in to a single provider's chat network
Trusted enough that the XMPP Standards Foundation runs its own infrastructure on it
cons: Requires system administration skills to install, configure, and maintain
No official commercial support contract from the project itself
User experience depends heavily on which XMPP client an end user chooses
Smaller mainstream awareness compared to closed commercial chat platforms

faqs.question: Is Prosody IM free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Prosody is free and open-source software with no licensing fees; the only costs involved are for the server infrastructure you choose to run it on.

faqs.question: What programming language is Prosody written in?
faqs.answer: Prosody is written in Lua, chosen to keep the server lightweight and easy to extend through plugins.

faqs.question: Who created Prosody IM?
faqs.answer: Prosody development was started by Matthew Wild in August 2008, with the first release (0.1.0) shipping in December 2008.

faqs.question: What protocol does Prosody use?
faqs.answer: Prosody implements XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol), also known as Jabber, an open, federated instant messaging standard.

faqs.question: Can Prosody communicate with other chat servers?
faqs.answer: Yes, because XMPP is federated like email, a Prosody server can exchange messages with any other standards-compliant XMPP server.

faqs.question: Does Prosody support encrypted messaging?
faqs.answer: Yes, Prosody works with end-to-end encryption protocols like OMEMO when used with compatible XMPP clients.

faqs.question: What is Snikket and how does it relate to Prosody?
faqs.answer: Snikket is a simplified, easier-to-deploy self-hosted chat distribution built on top of Prosody, created by the same lead developer, Matthew Wild.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused self-hosted team chat
use_cases.description: Organizations that want full control over their chat data run Prosody on their own servers instead of using a third-party commercial chat provider.

use_cases.title: Community and hobbyist XMPP servers
use_cases.description: Individuals and communities run Prosody to operate their own federated chat server that can communicate with the wider XMPP network.

use_cases.title: Standards and developer organizations
use_cases.description: Technical organizations, including the XMPP Standards Foundation itself, run Prosody to power internal team and meeting chatrooms.
use_cases.audience: Standards bodies and software development teams

features: Full server software under an open-source license
Self-hosted deployment on infrastructure of your choice
No usage limits imposed by the project

enrichment.short_description: Proton VPN is a Swiss-based, privacy-focused VPN service from Proton AG that offers a genuinely free tier alongside paid plans with high-speed servers, streaming access, and integration with Proton's broader privacy suite.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium with tiered monthly, annual, and 2-year subscription plans
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid VPN Plus plans from about $2.99/month billed every 2 years (or $9.99/month billed monthly)
languages: and additional languages (app localized in roughly 20-plus languages)
enrichment.seo_title: Proton VPN Review 2026: Pricing, Features, Pros and Cons
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Proton VPN's free and paid plans work in 2026, including pricing, server network, and how it fits into the Proton privacy ecosystem.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Proton VPN is a VPN service developed by Proton AG, a Swiss company founded in 2014 in Geneva by Andy Yen, Jason Stockman, and Wei Sun, researchers who met while working at CERN. Proton first launched as Proton Mail, an encrypted email service, and later expanded into a broader privacy suite; Proton VPN itself was released on May 22, 2017. The company is headquartered in Plan-les-Ouates, Geneva, Switzerland, and by the mid-2020s employed more than 500 people across more than 50 nationalities, having surpassed 100 million registered accounts across its products by 2024. Proton VPN is one of the few VPN services with a genuinely free tier rather than a time-limited trial: the free plan offers unlimited data with no artificial speed caps, though it restricts users to servers in a smaller set of countries (selected somewhat randomly) and secures only one device at a time. Paid tiers unlock the full network, reported at more than 20,400 servers across roughly 148 countries. The VPN Plus plan, which covers VPN service only, is priced starting around $2.99/month when billed on a 2-year cycle, roughly $3.99/month on a 1-year plan, or $9.99/month billed monthly; it secures up to 10 devices simultaneously, unlocks the fastest server speeds, adds an ad and malware blocker, and enables streaming access to services like Netflix, Disney+, and Prime Video. Proton Unlimited bundles VPN Plus with the rest of Proton's ecosystem, including Proton Mail, Proton Calendar, Proton Drive, Proton Pass, and Proton Docs and Sheets, priced from about $7.99/month on a 2-year plan up to $12.99/month billed monthly. Both paid tiers include a 30-day money-back guarantee. Proton VPN's apps are localized into roughly two dozen languages, including French, German, Spanish, Italian, Dutch, Polish, Portuguese, Russian, and Persian, reflecting Proton's global user base. As a Switzerland-domiciled company, Proton VPN operates under Swiss privacy law, which the company markets as a jurisdictional advantage since Switzerland sits outside the EU and US surveillance-sharing frameworks; Proton VPN also publishes its client apps' source code and has commissioned independent security audits to support its no-logs claims. The service competes directly with NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Surfshark, and differentiates itself primarily through its free-tier availability, integration with Proton's wider encrypted productivity suite, and its origin story tied to CERN researchers focused on digital privacy following the 2013 Snowden revelations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Proton VPN is developed by Proton AG, a Swiss privacy technology company founded in 2014 in Geneva by Andy Yen, Jason Stockman, and Wei Sun, who met as researchers at CERN. Proton began as Proton Mail, an encrypted email service, before expanding into a full privacy suite; Proton VPN itself launched on May 22, 2017. The company is headquartered in Plan-les-Ouates, Geneva, and by the mid-2020s employed more than 500 people representing over 50 nationalities, with Proton's products collectively surpassing 100 million registered accounts by 2024.

Because it operates under Swiss law, outside both EU and US data-sharing frameworks, Proton VPN markets its jurisdiction as a core part of its privacy positioning, alongside open-source client apps and independently audited no-logs claims.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Proton VPN's paid network spans more than 20,400 servers across roughly 148 countries, offering high-speed connections, streaming-optimized servers for services like Netflix and Disney+, and an integrated ad, tracker, and malware blocker. The service supports simultaneous connections on up to 10 devices on the VPN Plus plan.

Unusually for the VPN category, Proton VPN offers a genuinely free tier with unlimited data and no artificial speed throttling, rather than a time-limited trial, though the free tier limits users to a smaller set of server countries and one connected device at a time. Paid subscribers can also bundle VPN access with the rest of Proton's encrypted productivity suite through the Proton Unlimited plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Proton VPN offers a free-forever plan, plus two paid tiers. VPN Plus starts around $2.99/month billed every 2 years (about $3.99/month on a 1-year term, or $9.99/month billed monthly) and covers VPN service only. Proton Unlimited bundles VPN Plus with Proton Mail, Calendar, Drive, Pass, and Docs and Sheets, starting around $7.99/month on a 2-year plan up to $12.99/month billed monthly.
Both paid tiers include a 30-day money-back guarantee, and subscriptions can be billed monthly, annually, or on a 2-year cycle, with longer commitments unlocking the lowest effective monthly rate.

features.title: Free tier with unlimited data
features.description: Proton VPN's free plan has no data cap and no artificial speed limit, unlike most competitors' time-limited free trials.

features.title: Large global server network
features.description: Paid plans unlock a network of more than 20,400 servers across roughly 148 countries.

features.title: Streaming-optimized servers
features.description: Dedicated servers support streaming access to services including Netflix, Disney+, and Prime Video.

features.title: Ad, tracker, and malware blocking
features.description: Built-in NetShield feature blocks ads, trackers, and known malware domains at the network level.

features.title: Swiss jurisdiction and audited no-logs policy
features.description: Operates under Swiss privacy law and has commissioned independent audits to support its no-logs claims.

features.title: Multi-device support
features.description: VPN Plus secures up to 10 devices simultaneously across desktop, mobile, and browser apps.

features.title: Integration with the Proton ecosystem
features.description: Proton Unlimited bundles VPN access with Proton Mail, Calendar, Drive, Pass, and Docs and Sheets.

features.title: Open-source apps
features.description: Proton publishes the source code for its VPN client apps to support independent security review.

pros: Genuinely free plan with unlimited data, not just a limited trial
Large, fast server network on paid plans with strong streaming unblocking
Swiss jurisdiction outside EU/US surveillance-sharing frameworks
Bundles with the wider Proton privacy suite (Mail, Drive, Pass, Calendar)
Open-source apps and independently audited no-logs policy
cons: Free plan restricts device count to one and limits available server countries
Lowest advertised prices require committing to a 2-year billing cycle
Monthly billing is noticeably more expensive than annual or 2-year plans
Full ecosystem value requires the pricier Proton Unlimited tier rather than VPN Plus alone

faqs.question: Is Proton VPN really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Proton VPN offers a free plan with unlimited data and no artificial speed limit, though it restricts users to one device and a smaller set of server countries compared to paid plans.

faqs.question: How much does Proton VPN cost?
faqs.answer: VPN Plus starts around $2.99/month billed every 2 years (or $9.99/month billed monthly), while Proton Unlimited, which bundles the full Proton suite, starts around $7.99/month on a 2-year plan.

faqs.question: Who owns Proton VPN?
faqs.answer: Proton VPN is developed by Proton AG, a Swiss company founded in 2014 in Geneva by former CERN researchers Andy Yen, Jason Stockman, and Wei Sun.

faqs.question: How many servers does Proton VPN have?
faqs.answer: Proton VPN's paid network includes more than 20,400 servers across roughly 148 countries.

faqs.question: Does Proton VPN keep logs?
faqs.answer: Proton VPN states it operates a strict no-logs policy and has commissioned independent third-party audits to verify these claims, operating under Swiss privacy law.

faqs.question: Can Proton VPN unblock streaming services?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans include servers optimized for streaming access to services like Netflix, Disney+, and Prime Video.

faqs.question: Does Proton VPN work on multiple devices?
faqs.answer: Yes, the VPN Plus plan supports up to 10 simultaneous device connections across desktop, mobile, and browser apps.

Bundled pricing across the full Proton suite

enrichment.short_description: ProtoPie is a no-code interaction design tool that lets UX and product designers build highly realistic, code-free prototypes for mobile, desktop, web, and IoT interfaces.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium with per-editor monthly or annual subscription tiers
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans start at $25/month (Basic, 1 seat)
enrichment.seo_title: ProtoPie Review 2026: Pricing, Features, Pros and Cons
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore ProtoPie's Free, Basic, Pro, and Enterprise pricing plans and no-code prototyping features for UX and product design teams in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ProtoPie is a no-code interaction design and prototyping tool built by Studio XID, a company founded in 2014 in Seoul, South Korea by co-founders Tony Kim, Scotty Kim, and John Song. The company is headquartered in Seoul and, according to LinkedIn data, employs between 51 and 200 people; it has raised Series B funding as a venture-backed startup. ProtoPie is designed to let UX and product designers build highly interactive, production-like prototypes for mobile, desktop, web, and IoT interfaces without writing code, using a visual trigger-and-response system that can simulate gestures, sensors, device motion, and complex conditional logic beyond what typical design tools like Figma prototyping can achieve. The product is aimed at design teams that need to validate and user-test realistic interactions, such as animations, haptics, and hardware-triggered behavior, before handing designs off to engineering. Pricing follows a freemium model across four tiers. The Free plan supports two prototypes with up to two scenes each, 50MB of cloud storage, and one handoff recording, suitable for individual designers exploring the tool. The Basic plan costs $25/month for a single seat and removes the watermark while expanding to 20 prototypes, 10 scenes per prototype, 500MB of storage, and access to ProtoPie AI credits. The Pro plan, ProtoPie's most popular tier, costs $47 per editor per month (with up to 26 percent savings on annual billing) and unlocks unlimited prototypes, unlimited handoff recordings, team libraries, local prototype saving, and access to an annual-only user testing add-on. The Enterprise plan requires a minimum of three seats and offers custom pricing with a private server option, admin controls, dedicated training and support, custom hardware integrations, custom fonts, ISO compliance, and enterprise-grade security including single sign-on. ProtoPie also offers add-ons including Connect Core, priced at $20/month per user, which enables multi-screen interaction prototyping, plugin support, and custom integrations for teams building for connected devices. The company markets ProtoPie primarily to product design teams at technology companies, automotive and hardware manufacturers, and design agencies that need prototypes realistic enough to test real user behavior and gather stakeholder buy-in before development begins, differentiating itself from general-purpose design tools like Figma and Adobe XD through its focus on deep, sensor- and logic-driven interactivity rather than static screen-to-screen click-throughs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ProtoPie is a no-code interaction design tool built by Studio XID, a company founded in 2014 in Seoul, South Korea, by co-founders Tony Kim, Scotty Kim, and John Song. The company has raised Series B venture funding and, per LinkedIn, employs between 51 and 200 people, making it a mid-sized but globally used design tool vendor.

Rather than serving as a general-purpose UI design tool, ProtoPie focuses specifically on interaction and motion prototyping, letting designers simulate real device behavior such as gestures, sensors, and conditional logic without writing code, so that prototypes feel closer to a finished product than a static click-through mockup.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ProtoPie's core is a visual trigger-and-response system: designers define triggers (like a tap, drag, or device tilt) and responses (like an animation, sound, or state change) to build interactions without code. This extends to sensor-based prototyping, allowing designers to simulate accelerometer input, camera access, and other hardware-driven behavior relevant to mobile and IoT product design.

Team-oriented features on higher tiers include shared team libraries, local prototype saving, handoff recordings for developers, and the Connect Core add-on, which enables multi-screen and multi-device interaction prototyping useful for testing connected-device experiences like a phone app paired with a wearable or in-car display.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ProtoPie's Free plan supports two prototypes with limited scenes and storage, suitable for exploring the tool. The Basic plan is $25/month for one seat, and the Pro plan, ProtoPie's most popular tier, is $47 per editor per month with up to 26 percent savings when billed annually, unlocking unlimited prototypes and team collaboration features.

Enterprise pricing is custom and requires a minimum of three seats, adding private server deployment, SSO, and dedicated support. The Connect Core add-on for multi-screen prototyping is priced separately at $20/month per user.

features.title: No-code trigger-and-response interactions
features.description: Designers build complex interactions visually, defining triggers and responses without writing code.

features.title: Sensor and hardware simulation
features.description: Prototypes can respond to simulated device sensors like accelerometer, gyroscope, and camera input for mobile and IoT design.

features.description: The Connect Core add-on lets teams prototype interactions that span multiple connected screens or devices.

features.description: Pro and Enterprise plans support shared component libraries and collaborative editing across design teams.

features.description: Prototypes can be recorded and shared with engineers to communicate exact interaction timing and behavior.

features.description: Basic and Pro plans include AI-assisted credits to speed up prototype creation.

features.title: Enterprise security and private server option
features.description: Enterprise customers can deploy ProtoPie on a private server with SSO and ISO-compliant security controls.

features.title: User testing add-on
features.description: An annual-only add-on lets teams run structured user testing sessions directly against their prototypes.

pros: Enables far more realistic, hardware-aware prototypes than typical design tools
No-code interaction model is accessible to designers without engineering skills
Supports complex multi-screen and connected-device prototyping via Connect Core
Enterprise tier offers private server deployment for security-sensitive teams
Free plan available for individuals to evaluate the tool before purchasing
cons: Steeper learning curve than simpler click-through prototyping tools
Per-editor pricing on Pro and Enterprise can add up for larger design teams
Advanced features like Connect Core and user testing require paid add-ons
Enterprise plan requires a minimum of three seats, raising the entry cost for small security-focused teams

faqs.answer: Yes, ProtoPie offers a free plan supporting two prototypes with up to two scenes each, though paid plans are required for unlimited prototypes and team features.

faqs.answer: ProtoPie Pro costs $47 per editor per month, with up to 26 percent savings available on annual billing.

faqs.answer: ProtoPie is made by Studio XID, a company founded in 2014 in Seoul, South Korea by co-founders Tony Kim, Scotty Kim, and John Song.

faqs.answer: Yes, ProtoPie can simulate device sensors like accelerometer and gyroscope input, making it suitable for mobile and IoT product prototyping.

enrichment.short_description: ProWritingAid is an English-language writing assistant that combines grammar and style checking with more than 25 in-depth reports, aimed at authors, students, and professional writers who want deeper editing feedback than a basic grammar checker.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium with monthly, annual, or one-time lifetime purchase options
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; Premium from $10/month billed annually ($120/year), or $399 one-time lifetime purchase
languages: English (US, UK, Australian, Canadian, and General English variants)
enrichment.seo_title: ProWritingAid Review 2026: Pricing, Features, Pros and Cons
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Compare ProWritingAid's Free, Premium, and Premium Pro plans, including lifetime pricing, and see how its writing reports compare in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ProWritingAid is an English-language grammar and style checking tool founded by Chris Banks, who remains the company's CEO. The company is headquartered in Oxfordshire, England, with a registered office in London, and employs roughly 50 people as of the mid-2020s. Unlike broad multi-language grammar checkers, ProWritingAid supports English exclusively, but covers General, US, UK, Australian, and Canadian English variants, and it differentiates itself from lighter-weight tools like Grammarly by emphasizing deep, report-based feedback aimed particularly at long-form writers such as novelists, academics, and business writers rather than short-form everyday correspondence. The product's signature feature is a set of more than 25 in-depth writing reports covering areas like sentence length variation, overused words, pacing, cliches, passive voice, readability, and dialogue tags, designed to help writers revise their own prose rather than simply auto-correcting it. In 2026, ProWritingAid restructured its pricing, raising its long-standing annual plan from $79/year to $120/year, a roughly 52 percent increase, while introducing a new Premium Pro tier. The Free plan allows up to 500 words per submission and two report runs per day, with access to basic grammar, spelling, and punctuation checking plus limited daily rephrasing and Spark AI credits. The Premium plan costs $30/month, $120/year (equivalent to $10/month), or a one-time lifetime purchase of $399, and removes word and report-count limits while adding unlimited rephrasing, a custom style guide, a citations manager, an author comparison tool, and one daily AI chapter critique. Premium Pro costs $36/month, $144/year, or a $699 lifetime purchase, and builds on Premium with a higher daily AI Spark allowance, three daily chapter critiques, discounted Story Credits, access to live workshops and networking events with published authors and editors, and beta features like daily co-writing sprints and group critique sessions. Both paid lifetime tiers come with a 3-day money-back guarantee. ProWritingAid integrates as a browser extension, desktop app, and add-ins for Microsoft Word, Google Docs, and Scrivener, making it popular among fiction writers who draft in dedicated writing software rather than a browser. Its most direct competitor is Grammarly, and reviewers generally position ProWritingAid as offering deeper craft-level feedback for long-form and fiction writing, while Grammarly is often seen as faster and better suited to everyday business communication; even after the 2026 price increase, ProWritingAid's annual plan remains slightly cheaper than Grammarly Premium's $144/year list price.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ProWritingAid is an English-language writing assistant founded by CEO Chris Banks and headquartered in Oxfordshire, England, with a registered office in London. The company employs roughly 50 people and has built its product specifically around long-form writing, positioning it as a tool for novelists, academics, and business writers who want deeper craft feedback rather than just quick grammar corrections.

The tool supports General, US, UK, Australian, and Canadian English but does not currently support non-English languages, a deliberate scope decision that lets the company focus on depth of English-language analysis rather than breadth of language coverage.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ProWritingAid's core differentiator is a library of more than 25 in-depth writing reports that go beyond simple error flags, covering areas like sentence length variation, overused words, pacing, cliches, passive voice usage, dialogue tags, and overall readability. These reports are designed to teach writers about their own patterns and habits over time, not just correct individual mistakes.

The tool integrates as a browser extension and through native add-ins for Microsoft Word, Google Docs, and Scrivener, making it especially popular with fiction writers who draft inside dedicated writing software. Premium tiers add AI-powered rephrasing, chapter critiques, a citations manager, and an author comparison tool that benchmarks a writer's style against published authors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ProWritingAid restructured its pricing in 2026, raising its annual Premium plan from $79/year to $120/year (about $10/month) while introducing monthly ($30/month) and one-time lifetime ($399) purchase options. A new Premium Pro tier was also introduced at $36/month, $144/year, or a $699 lifetime purchase, adding higher AI usage limits and access to live author workshops.

A Free plan remains available with a 500-word submission limit and two report runs per day. Both paid lifetime purchases include a 3-day money-back guarantee, and even after the 2026 price increase, ProWritingAid's annual plan remains slightly below Grammarly Premium's $144/year list price.

Author comparison tool gives unique stylistic benchmarking not found in most competitors
One-time lifetime purchase option avoids ongoing subscription costs
Slightly cheaper annual pricing than Grammarly Premium even after the 2026 price increase

2026 pricing changes raised the annual plan by roughly 52 percent
Free plan is limited to 500 words per submission and two report runs per day
Report-heavy interface can feel overwhelming compared to simpler grammar checkers

faqs.question: How much does ProWritingAid cost?
faqs.answer: Premium costs $30/month, $120/year, or a $399 one-time lifetime purchase; Premium Pro costs $36/month, $144/year, or a $699 lifetime purchase.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of ProWritingAid?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Free plan allows up to 500 words per submission and two report runs per day with basic grammar and spelling checks.

faqs.question: Does ProWritingAid support languages other than English?
faqs.answer: No, ProWritingAid currently supports only English, including General, US, UK, Australian, and Canadian English variants.

faqs.question: Can I buy ProWritingAid as a one-time purchase?
faqs.answer: Yes, ProWritingAid offers lifetime purchase options at $399 for Premium and $699 for Premium Pro, with no recurring fees.

faqs.question: Does ProWritingAid work with Scrivener?
faqs.answer: Yes, ProWritingAid offers a native integration with Scrivener alongside Microsoft Word, Google Docs, and browser-based editing.

faqs.question: How is ProWritingAid different from Grammarly?
faqs.answer: ProWritingAid focuses on deep, report-based feedback for long-form and fiction writing, while Grammarly is generally viewed as faster and better suited to everyday business writing.

faqs.question: Who founded ProWritingAid?
faqs.answer: ProWritingAid was founded by Chris Banks, who remains the company's CEO, and is headquartered in Oxfordshire, England.

enrichment.short_description: Proxmox Backup Server is an open-source enterprise backup solution for backing up and restoring virtual machines, containers, and physical hosts, built on Rust with deduplication and incremental-forever backups.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free open-source download with optional paid annual per-server support subscriptions
enrichment.starting_price: Free to use without subscription; paid support subscriptions start at 560 euros/server/year (Community tier)
languages: English (primary interface); community-translated documentation and forum support in multiple languages
enrichment.seo_title: Proxmox Backup Server Review 2026: Pricing, Features, Pros and Cons
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Proxmox Backup Server's free and subscription tiers work in 2026, including deduplication, incremental backups, and enterprise support pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Proxmox Backup Server is an open-source, enterprise-grade backup solution developed by Proxmox Server Solutions GmbH, a company founded in 2005 in Vienna, Austria by Martin Maurer and Dietmar Maurer. The company, best known for its flagship Proxmox Virtual Environment (Proxmox VE) hypervisor platform, employs roughly 50 to 54 people according to recent estimates. Proxmox Backup Server is a distinct product from Proxmox VE, focused specifically on backing up and restoring virtual machines, containers, and physical hosts rather than on virtualization itself, though the two products integrate tightly. Development of Proxmox Backup began in October 2018 to provide a more efficient, purpose-built backup mechanism for the Proxmox VE ecosystem; the first public beta was announced in July 2020, and the first stable 1.0 release followed in November 2020. The product is built entirely on Rust, a systems programming language chosen for its performance and memory safety, and its core technical advantages include client-side deduplication, incremental-forever backups (meaning only changed data blocks are transferred after the initial full backup), and built-in encryption. Like the rest of the Proxmox product line, Proxmox Backup Server is released under the GNU Affero General Public License (AGPLv3) and can be downloaded and used entirely free of charge through the no-subscription repository, with unlimited backup storage and unlimited backup clients even without a paid subscription; this has made it especially popular among home-lab users and smaller, cost-conscious IT teams. For organizations that want access to the more thoroughly tested Enterprise Repository and official technical support, Proxmox offers four paid annual subscription tiers priced per server per year: Community at 560 euros includes community-only support with no ticketed support; Basic at 1,120 euros includes 5 support tickets per year with one-business-day response; Standard at 2,240 euros, described as the most popular tier, includes 15 support tickets per year with 4-hour response time and remote SSH support; and Premium at 4,480 euros includes unlimited support tickets, 2-hour response time for critical issues, remote SSH support, and offline subscription key activation for air-gapped environments. All subscription tiers include unlimited backup storage and unlimited backup clients regardless of tier, with the paid tiers differentiated purely by support level and repository access rather than by feature restrictions. Proxmox Backup Server has progressed through major version releases including 2.x and 3.x, with version 4.0 released in August 2025, 4.1 in November 2025, and 4.2 in April 2026. It is commonly deployed alongside Proxmox VE in homelab, SMB, and enterprise environments as a self-hosted alternative to commercial backup products like Veeam, differentiating itself primarily through its fully-featured free tier and transparent, feature-inclusive subscription pricing model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Proxmox Backup Server is an open-source enterprise backup product developed by Proxmox Server Solutions GmbH, founded in 2005 in Vienna, Austria by Martin Maurer and Dietmar Maurer. While the company is best known for Proxmox Virtual Environment, its virtualization platform, Proxmox Backup Server is a distinct, purpose-built product focused specifically on backing up and restoring virtual machines, containers, and physical hosts.

Development began in October 2018, with a public beta in July 2020 and the first stable 1.0 release in November 2020. Built entirely on Rust for performance and memory safety, the product has since progressed through several major versions, including version 4.0 in August 2025 and 4.2 in April 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Proxmox Backup Server's core technical strengths are client-side deduplication and an incremental-forever backup model, meaning that after an initial full backup, only changed data blocks are transferred and stored, significantly reducing both backup time and storage consumption. Backups can be encrypted client-side before they ever reach the backup server, and the product integrates tightly with Proxmox VE for consistent VM and container snapshots.

Because it is open source under the AGPLv3 license, Proxmox Backup Server can be downloaded and run entirely free of charge with unlimited backup storage and unlimited backup clients, even without a paid subscription, via the community no-subscription repository. Paid subscribers gain access to the more thoroughly tested Proxmox Enterprise Repository and tiered technical support rather than any additional product features.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Proxmox Backup Server itself is free to download and use without any subscription, with no artificial limits on storage or number of backup clients. Organizations that want official support and access to the Enterprise Repository can purchase one of four annual per-server subscription tiers: Community at 560 euros/server/year (community support only), Basic at 1,120 euros (5 tickets/year), Standard at 2,240 euros (15 tickets/year, the most popular tier), and Premium at 4,480 euros/server/year (unlimited tickets, 2-hour response).

All subscription tiers include the same core feature set — unlimited backup storage and unlimited backup clients — with pricing differentiated entirely by support ticket volume, response time commitments, and access to remote SSH support or offline activation for air-gapped environments.

features.title: Incremental-forever backups
features.description: Only changed data blocks are backed up after the initial full backup, reducing backup windows and storage use over time.

features.title: Client-side deduplication
features.description: Deduplicates data before it is transferred to the backup server, cutting down on both bandwidth and storage requirements.

features.title: Built-in encryption
features.description: Supports client-side encryption so backup data can be encrypted before it leaves the source machine.

features.title: Tight Proxmox VE integration
features.description: Works natively with Proxmox Virtual Environment for consistent backups of virtual machines and containers.

features.title: Free, fully-featured community edition
features.description: The no-subscription repository provides the complete feature set, including unlimited storage and clients, at no cost.

features.title: Rust-based architecture
features.description: Built entirely in Rust for performance, reliability, and memory safety compared to older backup tooling.

features.title: Tiered enterprise support subscriptions
features.description: Four paid support tiers offer increasing ticket volume, response times, and remote SSH support without restricting features.

features.title: Backup of physical hosts and containers
features.description: Supports backing up not just VMs but also physical (bare-metal) hosts and Linux containers.

pros: Fully-featured free tier with unlimited storage and backup clients, not a crippled trial
Efficient incremental-forever backups with client-side deduplication reduce storage costs
Open-source under AGPLv3 with no vendor lock-in
Tight, native integration with Proxmox Virtual Environment
Subscription tiers differentiate by support level only, not by withholding features
cons: Official technical support requires a paid annual subscription per server
Backup licensing costs can exceed Proxmox VE hypervisor licensing costs on the same infrastructure
Best suited to organizations already using or willing to adopt Proxmox VE and the broader open-source stack
Enterprise Repository access is gated behind a paid subscription, unlike the free no-subscription repository

faqs.question: Is Proxmox Backup Server free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be downloaded and used entirely free of charge via the no-subscription repository, with unlimited backup storage and unlimited backup clients.

faqs.question: What does a Proxmox Backup Server subscription include?
faqs.answer: Paid subscriptions provide access to the more thoroughly tested Proxmox Enterprise Repository and tiered technical support, ranging from community-only support up to unlimited tickets with 2-hour response times on the Premium tier.

faqs.question: How much do Proxmox Backup Server subscriptions cost?
faqs.answer: Subscriptions are priced per server per year: Community at 560 euros, Basic at 1,120 euros, Standard at 2,240 euros, and Premium at 4,480 euros.

faqs.question: What programming language is Proxmox Backup Server built in?
faqs.answer: It is built entirely on Rust, chosen for performance and memory safety.

faqs.question: Who develops Proxmox Backup Server?
faqs.answer: It is developed by Proxmox Server Solutions GmbH, founded in 2005 in Vienna, Austria by Martin Maurer and Dietmar Maurer.

faqs.question: Does Proxmox Backup Server support deduplication?
faqs.answer: Yes, it performs client-side deduplication combined with an incremental-forever backup model to minimize storage and bandwidth use.

faqs.question: Is Proxmox Backup Server the same as Proxmox Virtual Environment?
faqs.answer: No, they are distinct products; Proxmox VE is a virtualization/hypervisor platform, while Proxmox Backup Server is a dedicated backup solution that integrates with it.

use_cases.title: Homelab and small IT teams needing free enterprise backup
use_cases.description: Cost-conscious administrators run the no-subscription edition to get deduplicated, incremental-forever backups without licensing fees.
use_cases.audience: Homelab users and small IT teams

use_cases.title: Enterprises needing supported production backup infrastructure
use_cases.description: Organizations running production Proxmox VE clusters purchase Standard or Premium subscriptions for guaranteed support response times.

use_cases.title: Regulated or air-gapped environments
use_cases.description: Teams operating in secure or offline environments use offline subscription key activation available on Standard and Premium tiers.
use_cases.audience: Regulated industries and air-gapped data centers
